title: Utility and expected decisions

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Utility and expected decisions; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Planning Systems, Constraint Satisfaction, Decision Making, and Reinforcement Learning foundations; atomic topic: Utility and expected decisions
sources.claim: Expected utility compares actions by summing each modeled outcome's utility weighted by its probability, exposing value judgments, uncertainty, and sensitivity to assumptions.

sources.claim: Expected utility compares actions by summing each modeled outcome's utility weighted by its probability, exposing value judgments, uncertainty, and sensitivity to assumptions.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Expected utility compares actions by summing each modeled outcome's utility weighted by its probability, exposing value judgments, uncertainty, and sensitivity to assumptions.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Two treatments are compared using benefit, side-effect utility, and calibrated scenario probabilities, followed by a sensitivity range for the uncertain harm rate. Your goal is to compute an expected-utility choice and critique sensitivity to its assumptions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: State-space and adversarial search; Knowledge representation and uncertainty; Conditional probability and Bayes rule. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Expected utility compares actions by summing each modeled outcome's utility weighted by its probability, exposing value judgments, uncertainty, and sensitivity to assumptions. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Utility is stakeholder-dependent, probabilities can be poorly estimated, rare severe harms can dominate, expected value can hide distribution and fairness, and constraints may rule out an otherwise high score. The learner must reject this overclaim: The action with the largest expected monetary value is objectively rational for every stakeholder and ethical context.

includes: Compare actions by outcome utility and probability while making value choices explicit.
Compute an expected-utility choice and critique sensitivity to its assumptions.
excludes: Utility is stakeholder-dependent, probabilities can be poorly estimated, rare severe harms can dominate, expected value can hide distribution and fairness, and constraints may rule out an otherwise high score.

outcomes: Compute an expected-utility choice and critique sensitivity to its assumptions.

examples.title: Utility and expected decisions: bounded direct case
examples.situation: Two treatments are compared using benefit, side-effect utility, and calibrated scenario probabilities, followed by a sensitivity range for the uncertain harm rate.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: Two treatments are compared using benefit, side-effect utility, and calibrated scenario probabilities, followed by a sensitivity range for the uncertain harm rate.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Make outcome utilities, probabilities, stakeholders, constraints, and sensitivity explicit; expected utility supports a bounded choice rather than neutralizing values.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Make outcome utilities, probabilities, stakeholders, constraints, and sensitivity explicit; expected utility supports a bounded choice rather than neutralizing values.

examples.title: Utility and expected decisions: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: An action with slightly higher expected utility is rejected because it violates an explicit safety constraint for one affected group.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: An action with slightly higher expected utility is rejected because it violates an explicit safety constraint for one affected group.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Utility is stakeholder-dependent, probabilities can be poorly estimated, rare severe harms can dominate, expected value can hide distribution and fairness, and constraints may rule out an otherwise high score.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: The action with the largest expected monetary value is objectively rational for every stakeholder and ethical context.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Compute an expected-utility choice and critique sensitivity to its assumptions.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The action with the largest expected monetary value is objectively rational for every stakeholder and ethical context.
misconceptions.correction: Make outcome utilities, probabilities, stakeholders, constraints, and sensitivity explicit; expected utility supports a bounded choice rather than neutralizing values.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: The action with the largest expected monetary value is objectively rational for every stakeholder and ethical context.

analogy.example: a bounded operations desk that checks action preconditions, resource constraints, uncertain outcomes, and proxy rewards before acting can introduce Utility and expected decisions only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.

recap.summary: You should now be able to compute an expected-utility choice and critique sensitivity to its assumptions. Keep this boundary in view: Utility is stakeholder-dependent, probabilities can be poorly estimated, rare severe harms can dominate, expected value can hide distribution and fairness, and constraints may rule out an otherwise high score.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Utility and expected decisions with one technical mechanism, one evidence requirement, and one non-claim.
Why is this overclaim invalid: The action with the largest expected monetary value is objectively rational for every stakeholder and ethical context.
Apply the boundary to: An action with slightly higher expected utility is rejected because it violates an explicit safety constraint for one affected group.
